---
title: "Exercise: Your first SQLite database"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Open the `sqlite3` CLI, create a `strain` table, load 10 microbial strains into it from a CSV, and run a handful of `SELECT` queries. By the end of this exercise you will have used [`CREATE TABLE`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_createtable.html), [`INSERT`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_insert.html), [`SELECT`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_select.html) with [`WHERE`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_select.html#whereclause), [`ORDER BY`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_select.html#orderby), [`LIMIT`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_select.html#limitoffset), [`GROUP BY`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_select.html#groupby) and [`COUNT(*)`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_aggfunc.html#count) — the parts of SQL you will use ten times a week for the rest of your career.

If you have never written SQL before, **this is the right starting point**. We do not assume any prior database experience.

If you have not yet installed `sqlite3`, do that first — see [Installing SQLite](../intro/install-sqlite.qmd).

## Background

A microbial strain collection is a natural fit for a relational database. We want one row per strain with columns:

| Column | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `strain_id` | INTEGER | unique identifier — the **primary key** |
| `strain_name` | TEXT | the common laboratory name |
| `species` | TEXT | binomial name |
| `isolation_source` | TEXT | where it was originally isolated from |
| `year_isolated` | INTEGER | year the strain was first described |

The starter ships with 10 strains in [`data/strains.csv`](ex-sqlite-strains/data/strains.csv). Take a moment to look at the file — it is plain CSV, header line on top.

![The schema describes the *shape* of every row in the `strain` table — the column names, types, and which constraints apply. The primary key (`strain_id`) uniquely identifies each row and is assigned automatically when a row is inserted. The card on the right shows what one actual row looks like once that schema is filled in.](images/strain-schema.svg){#fig-strain-schema fig-alt="Diagram of the strain table schema: a card with a light-blue header showing the table name, then one row per column listing strain_id (INTEGER, primary key, marked with a green PK badge), strain_name (TEXT, NOT NULL), species (TEXT, NOT NULL), isolation_source (TEXT), and year_isolated (INTEGER). To the right, a sample row showing strain_id=1, strain_name='K-12 MG1655', species='Escherichia coli', isolation_source='human gut', year_isolated=1922."}

## The exercise

```bash
cd day7/ex-sqlite-strains
sqlite3 strains.db
```

`sqlite3` opens (creating `strains.db` because it does not exist yet) and gives you a `sqlite>` prompt. Everything below happens at that prompt unless noted.

### Step 1 — settings

Set two display options so output is actually readable:

```sql
.headers on
.mode column
```

These are **dot-commands** (no leading `SELECT`, no trailing `;`) interpreted by the CLI itself — see [`sqlite3` docs](https://www.sqlite.org/cli.html).

### Step 2 — create the table

Run the `CREATE TABLE` statement from the *Background* table above. The syntax:

```sql
CREATE TABLE strain (
    strain_id        INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    strain_name      TEXT    NOT NULL,
    species          TEXT    NOT NULL,
    isolation_source TEXT,
    year_isolated    INTEGER
);
```

Then verify with two dot-commands:

```
.tables          -- lists table names; should show "strain"
.schema strain   -- shows the CREATE TABLE you just ran
```

### Step 3 — insert one row by hand

```sql
INSERT INTO strain (strain_name, species, isolation_source, year_isolated)
VALUES ('K-12 MG1655', 'Escherichia coli', 'human gut', 1922);

SELECT * FROM strain;
```

Single quotes around text. Integers bare. The `strain_id` was assigned automatically because the column is `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`.

### Step 4 — bulk-load from CSV

Loading nine more rows by hand would be tedious. SQLite's CLI can read CSV directly:

```
.mode csv
.import --skip 1 data/strains.csv strain
.mode column
```

`.import` reads each line of the CSV into the named table; `--skip 1` skips the header. Switch back to column mode for readable output.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="The first row I inserted is now there twice"}
The CSV includes row 1 (`K-12 MG1655`) too. Either drop the table and start over (`DROP TABLE strain;` then redo Step 2 + Step 4), or skip Step 3's manual insert and only do the CSV load. Both are fine.
:::

Verify:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM strain;       -- should be 10 (or 11 if duplicated)
SELECT * FROM strain LIMIT 3;
```

### Step 5 — queries

Now the part you'll actually use. Run each of these and read the output:

```sql
-- All E. coli strains:
SELECT strain_name, year_isolated
FROM strain
WHERE species = 'Escherichia coli';

-- The five most recently isolated strains:
SELECT strain_name, species, year_isolated
FROM strain
ORDER BY year_isolated DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- How many strains per species?
SELECT species, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM strain
GROUP BY species
ORDER BY n DESC;

-- Just the species names, no duplicates:
SELECT DISTINCT species FROM strain;
```

Make sure you understand the difference between `WHERE` (filter rows *before* aggregation) and `GROUP BY` (bucket the remaining rows and aggregate within each bucket).

### Step 6 — write a query yourself

Write SQL that answers:

> *"Which strains have an `isolation_source` containing the word "lab"?"*

You will want the [`LIKE`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_expr.html#like) operator. The wildcard is `%`.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
```sql
SELECT strain_name, species, isolation_source
FROM strain
WHERE isolation_source LIKE '%lab%';
```

Four strains match: `DH5alpha`, `BL21(DE3)`, `W3110`, all *E. coli* lab derivatives.
:::

### Step 7 — leave

```
.quit
```

Your `strains.db` file is still on disk. Open it again any time with `sqlite3 strains.db` and your data is right there — no server, no daemon, no setup.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — I got 'Error: incomplete input'"}
You forgot the trailing `;`. SQLite waits patiently until it sees one before running your statement. The prompt changes from `sqlite>` to `   ...>` to remind you it is in the middle of a statement.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — single vs double quotes"}
Single quotes (`'foo'`) wrap **string values**. Double quotes (`"foo"`) wrap **identifiers** — column or table names. If you write `WHERE species = "Escherichia coli"`, SQLite looks for a column called `Escherichia coli` and gives you a confusing error. Always use single quotes for text values.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — start over from scratch"}
Easiest: exit, delete the file, re-open:

```bash
.quit
rm strains.db
sqlite3 strains.db
```

Or run the full pre-built seed file from the shell:

```bash
sqlite3 strains.db < data/seed.sql
```

That gives you both tables fully populated in one shot, which is the cleanest starting point for [exercise 02](02-natural-join.qmd).
:::

## Where this leads

- [**Exercise 02**](02-natural-join.qmd) — add a second table and join the two with `NATURAL JOIN`.
- [**Exercises 03–05**](03-read-from-rust.qmd) — read and write the same `strains.db` from Rust using [`rusqlite`](https://docs.rs/rusqlite/).
- Real strain collections (e.g. [BiGG Models](http://bigg.ucsd.edu/), [PATRIC/BV-BRC](https://www.bv-brc.org/)) have hundreds of columns and millions of rows but the queries you wrote today still apply unchanged.
